title: Green-wood joinery: the basics
subtitle: How green wood moves as it dries, and how to design joints that use that movement
excerpt: Green-wood joinery relies on the fact that wood shrinks across the grain as it dries but very little along it. Round tenons driven into wet mortises tighten as the mortise shrinks; drawbore pins lock the joint before it dries. This reading covers the mechanics, the main joint types, and the working sequence.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the green-wood joint types; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for the drawbore method.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Shrinks significantly across the grain as it dries.
Split along the grain with a froe and maul. Riven stock has straight grain and greater strength than sawn stock.
A locking method for mortise-and-tenon joints: a hole is bored through the mortise wall and then offset through the tenon so that driving a peg draws the shoulder tight.
A recess or hole in a piece of wood that receives the tenon of a mating part.

content.text: Wood shrinks as it dries, but not evenly in all directions. Along the length of the grain, shrinkage is almost nothing: a 400 mm leg will still be 400 mm dry. Across the grain, shrinkage is significant: a 40 mm round tenon may dry to 37 or 38 mm.
Green-wood
joinery exploits this. A round tenon driven into a wet
mortise
tightens as the mortise shrinks around it. A
drawbore
pin driven through the joint before drying pulls the shoulder tight and resists later loosening.
The round tenon and tapered mortise
The round tenon is the most common green-wood joint. A leg or stretcher is shaped at the shaving horse to a consistent cylinder at one end; that cylinder is the tenon. A corresponding round hole, the
mortise

, is bored into the seat or rail. The tenon is slightly larger than the mortise: 40 mm tenon into a 38 mm mortise is a typical fit. The fit is tight enough to require a beetle blow to drive the joint home.

The mortise is sometimes bored at a splay angle to give the finished piece stability. A chair leg splays outward 6 to 10 degrees; this means boring the mortise off-perpendicular from the seat face. Consistent splay across all four legs requires a bevel gauge set to the target angle before drilling begins.
Green on green versus green into dry

Two strategies exist. In green-on-green assembly, both tenon and mortise are wet when assembled. The mortise shrinks onto the tenon as both dry. This is the traditional Windsor chair method and produces the tightest possible joint over time.

In green-into-dry assembly, the mortise piece (usually the seat or frame) has been dried first; only the tenon piece is green. As the tenon dries it shrinks slightly across the grain, which means the joint loosens unless a drawbore pin is used. This method is faster but requires drawboring without exception.
The drawbore pin
Drawboring works by offsetting a hole between the mortise wall and the tenon. With the tenon dry-fitted into the
mortise

, a hole is bored through the mortise piece at a point that passes through the tenon. The tenon is then withdrawn and a second hole is bored in the tenon alone, shifted 3 to 4 mm toward the tenon shoulder from the first bore mark. When the tenon is re-inserted and a tapered peg driven through, the offset draws the shoulder tight. A
riven
oak peg is best: it is strong enough in the long grain to take the draw force without snapping.
Wedged tenons

A wedged tenon is an alternative to drawboring. A sawcut is made across the tenon end before driving. Two thin wedges of dry hardwood are tapped into the sawcut after the tenon is home; the wedges spread the tenon end inside the mortise and lock it mechanically. This method requires the mortise to be slightly flared at the blind end to allow the spread. Wedged tenons are used in stretchers and handles where boring a drawbore hole is impractical.
Working sequence
Prepare and dry the mortise piece first if using green-into-dry. The seat, rail, or frame should be at equilibrium moisture content before the tenon legs are fitted.
Shape the tenons last. Cut and shape the tenon legs from green stock as late as possible in the build sequence so they are still wet when driven.
Check splay angles on a test piece. A 1-degree error on a 400 mm leg shifts the foot position by 7 mm.
Drive all tenons home in one session. The tenon fit begins to loosen within hours as surface moisture equalises; once assembly starts, complete it.
Re-check and re-drive after two weeks. The first two weeks of drying release the most stress. Check each joint and re-drive any that feel loose with the beetle.
